elements.sent-2411-001: This study introduces a debiasing method for regression estimators, including high-dimensional and nonparametric regression estimators.
elements.sent-2411-002: For example, nonparametric regression methods allow for the estimation of regression functions in a data-driven manner with minimal assumptions; however, these methods typically fail to achieve $\sqrt{n}$-consistency in their convergence rates, and many, including those in machine learning, lack guarantees that their estimators asymptotically follow a normal distribution.
elements.sent-2411-003: To address these challenges, we propose a debiasing technique for nonparametric estimators by adding a bias-correction term to the original estimators, extending the conventional one-step estimator used in semiparametric analysis.
elements.sent-2411-004: Specifically, for each data point, we estimate the conditional expected residual of the original nonparametric estimator, which can, for instance, be computed using kernel (Nadaraya-Watson) regression, and incorporate it as a bias-reduction term.
elements.sent-2411-005: Our theoretical analysis demonstrates that the proposed estimator achieves $\sqrt{n}$-consistency and asymptotic normality under a mild convergence rate condition for both the original nonparametric estimator and the conditional expected residual estimator.
elements.sent-2411-006: Notably, this approach remains model-free as long as the original estimator and the conditional expected residual estimator satisfy the convergence rate condition.
elements.sent-2411-007: The proposed method offers several advantages, including improved estimation accuracy and simplified construction of confidence intervals.
elements.sent-2411-008: We consider $d$-dimensional random covariates $\bmX \in \calX \subset \bbR^d$ and a response variable $Y \in \calY \subset \bbR$, where $\calX$ and $\calY$ denote the covariate and response variable spaces, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-009: The pair of random variables $(\bmX, Y)$ is generated according to the following regression model: \begin{equation}Y = f_0(\bmX) + \varepsilon,\end{equation} where $f_0(\bmX) \coloneqq \bbE\sqb{Y \mid \bmX}$ is the regression function, and $\varepsilon \in \bbR$ is a mean-zero error term with the conditional variance $\sigma^2_0(\bmX)$ given $\bmX \in \calX$.
elements.sent-2411-010: We denote the joint distribution of $(\bmX, Y)$ by $P_0$ and assume it has the density $p_0(\bmx, y) = \zeta_0(\bmx)q_0(y \mid \bmx)$ with respect to some Lebesgue measure, where $\zeta_0(\bmx)$ is the marginal density of $\bmX$ and $q_0$ is the conditional density of $Y$ given $\bmX = \bmx$.
elements.sent-2411-011: Given observations $\calD \coloneqq \{(\bmX_i, Y_i)\}_{i=1 ^n$, which are i.i.d.}
elements.sent-2411-012: The goal is to obtain an estimator of $f_0(\bmx)$ that achieves $\sqrt{n}$-consistency and asymptotic normality.
elements.sent-2411-013: We allow the high-dimensional setting and the nonparametric setting, where we do not impose parametric assumptions on $f_0$.
elements.sent-2411-014: Several approaches have been developed to estimate $f_0$.
elements.sent-2411-015: The simplest approach assumes a linear model for $f_0(\bmx)$, such as $\bmx^\top \bm{\gamma}$.
elements.sent-2411-016: In this case, $f_0$ can be estimated by the ordinary least squares (OLS) method, which yields $\sqrt{n}$-consistent estimators that asymptotically follow a normal distribution.
elements.sent-2411-017: To relax the assumptions on the regression model, nonparametric regression methods have been widely employed \citep{Györfi2002,Tsybakov2008,Wasserman2010 .}
elements.sent-2411-018: However, it is well-known that estimators obtained from nonparametric regression typically fail to achieve $\sqrt{n}$-consistency, as evidenced by the minimax lower bounds for nonparametric regression function estimation \citep{Stone1977,Stone1982 .}
elements.sent-2411-019: The lack of $\sqrt{n}$-consistency implies not only slower convergence rates compared to parametric estimators but also increased challenges in conducting statistical inference \citep{Li1989 .}
elements.sent-2411-020: This slow convergence rate arises partly due to the bias (or approximation error) inherent in nonparametric regression models.
elements.sent-2411-021: Furthermore, in high-dimensional regression settings, such as Lasso regression, estimators also fail to achieve $\sqrt{n}$-consistency due to bias terms that diminish at a rate slower than $\sqrt{n}$ as the sample size increases.
elements.sent-2411-022: In this study, we develop a debiased $\sqrt{n}$-consistent estimator for $f_0(\bmx)$ in the general setting of regression, encompassing high-dimensional and nonparametric regression.
elements.sent-2411-023: We consider the case where a regression estimator $\widehat{f}\colon \calX \to \calY$ is given, which is consistent but not $\sqrt{n}$-consistent.
elements.sent-2411-024: We also do not require the asymptotic normality of the original estimator.
elements.sent-2411-025: Based on this original estimator, to achieve $\sqrt{n}$-consistency, we debias the estimator by adding a debiasing term $\widehat{h _n(\bmx)$, which is an estimator of the conditional expected residual of the original estimator, $\bbE\sqb{Y - \widehat{f (\bmX) \mid \bmX = \bmx}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-026: This estimator of the conditional expected residual is required to be constructed as a weighted average estimator of $Y_i$, expressed as $\sum^n_{i=1 \widehat{w _{n, \bmx (\bmX_i) \p{Y_i - \widehat{f}\p{\bmX_i }$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-027: Then, we obtain the debiased estimator $\widetilde{f (\bmx) = \widehat{f (\bmx) + \widehat{h _n(\bmx)$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-028: For the proposed debiased estimator $\widetilde{f _n$, we prove that if the original regression estimator $\widehat{f _n$ and the conditional expected residual estimator $\widehat{h _n$ are consistent and satisfy mild convergence-rate assumptions, then the pointwise asymptotic normality $\sqrt{n}\p{\widetilde{f _n(\bmx) - f_0(\bmx) \xrightarrow{\rmd \calN\p{0, \omega}$ holds.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-029: The concept behind the debiased estimator $\widetilde{f _n(\bmx)$ is to update the original estimator $\widehat{f _n(\bmx)$ in a direction that reduces its bias.}}
elements.sent-2411-030: This concept is illustrated in Figure~\ref{fig:gradientdescent .}
elements.sent-2411-031: The $\sqrt{n}$-consistency of our approach offers significant advantages.
elements.sent-2411-032: In nonparametric regression, traditional methods for constructing confidence intervals often rely on unknown biases in the regression function \citep{Li1989,Armstrong2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-033: By contrast, our method enables straightforward construction of confidence intervals based on $\sqrt{n}$-consistency.
elements.sent-2411-034: Additionally, we derive the semiparametric efficiency bound, which represents the lower bound of the asymptotic variance for regular and asymptotically linear (RAL) conditional mean estimators.
elements.sent-2411-035: Our analysis shows that the debiased estimator is efficient, as its asymptotic variance aligns with this bound.

elements.sent-2411-043: The design of our estimator and its theoretical analysis are closely related to semiparametric theory \citep{bickel1998efficient,Vaart1998 .}
elements.sent-2411-044: In semiparametric theory, for a parameter of interest, the asymptotic variance of RAL estimators is lower bounded by the variance of the efficient influence function.
elements.sent-2411-045: The goal is to construct an estimator that belongs to the class of RAL estimators and achieves the efficiency bound by aligning its asymptotic variance with the derived lower bound.
elements.sent-2411-046: A standard approach for constructing efficient estimators involves applying bias correction to an initial inefficient estimator, leveraging knowledge of the efficient influence function \citep{Schuler2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-047: This procedure is analogous to a one-step update in gradient descent optimization, where the efficient influence function serves as the gradient.
elements.sent-2411-048: Consequently, these bias-correction methods are often referred to as one-step approaches \citep{vanderVaart2002 .}
elements.sent-2411-049: Our debiased estimator is also constructed based on this idea.
elements.sent-2411-050: The debiased estimator is also inspired by doubly robust estimators in causal inference \citep{BangRobins2005 .}
elements.sent-2411-051: Doubly robust estimators are not only valuable for constructing efficient estimators but also ensure double robustness.
elements.sent-2411-052: Specifically, if either the original estimator or the debiasing term is consistent, the resulting debiased estimator is also consistent, even if it does not exhibit asymptotic normality.
elements.sent-2411-053: This robustness is beneficial in practice since it guarantees the validity of the estimator.

elements.formula-2411-074: \begin{equation}\left|\bbE\Big[\phi\p{X, Y; f_0, w_{0, \bmx}} - \phi\p{X, Y; \widehat{f}_n, \widehat{w}_{n, \bmx}} \mid \widehat{f}_n, \widehat{w}_{n, \bmx}\Big]\right| = \Big|\bbE\Big[\Big(Y - f_0(\bmX)\Big)w_{0, \bmx}(\bmX) + f_0(\bmx) - \Big(Y - \widehat{f}_n(\bmX)\Big)\widehat{w}_{n, \bmx}(\bmX) - \widehat{f}_n(\bmx) \mid \widehat{f}_n, \widehat{w}_{n, \bmx}\Big]\Big| = \Big|\bbE\Big[\Big(\widehat{f}_n(\bmX) - f_0(\bmX)\Big)\widehat{w}_{n, \bmx}(\bmX) + f_0(\bmx) - \widehat{f}_n(\bmx) \mid \widehat{f}_n, \widehat{w}_{n, \bmx}\Big]\Big| = \Big|\bbE\Big[\Big(\widehat{f}_n(\bmX) - f_0(\bmX)\Big)\widehat{w}_{n, \bmx}(\bmX) + \Big(f_0(\bmX) - \widehat{f}_n(\bmX)\Big)w_{0, \bmx}(\bmX) \mid \widehat{f}_n, \widehat{w}_{n, \bmx}\Big]\Big| = \left|\bbE\sqb{\Big(\widehat{f}_n(\bmX) - f_0(\bmX)\Big)\p{\frac{K\p{(\bmX_i - \bmx)/ h_n}}{\widehat{\zeta}_n(\bmx)} - \frac{\xi_v\big(\bmX - \bmx\big)}{\zeta_0(\bmx)}} \mid \widehat{f}_n, \widehat{w}_{n, \bmx}}\right| = \Bigg|\bbE\Bigg[\Big(\widehat{f}_n(\bmX) - f_0(\bmX)\Big)\Bigg(\frac{K\p{(\bmX_i - \bmx)/ h_n}}{\widehat{\zeta}_n(\bmx)} - \frac{K\p{(\bmX_i - \bmx)/ h_n}}{\zeta_0(\bmx)} \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ + \frac{K\p{(\bmX_i - \bmx)/ h_n}}{\zeta_0(\bmx)} - \frac{\xi_v\big(\bmX - \bmx\big)}{\zeta_0(\bmx)}\Bigg) \mid \widehat{f}_n, \widehat{w}_{n, \bmx}\Bigg]\Bigg|.\end{equation}

elements.sent-2411-268: From Assumption~\ref{asm:convergence_rate and the definition of $w_{0, \bmx, v}$, we have \begin{equation}\lim_{v\to 0}\lim_{n\to \infty}\sqrt{n}\left|\bbE\Big[\phi\p{X, Y; f_0, w_{0, \bmx, v}} - \phi\p{X, Y; \widehat{f}_n, \widehat{w}_{n, \bmx}} \mid \widehat{f}_n, \widehat{w}_{n, \bmx}\Big]\right| \leq \lim_{v\to 0}\lim_{n\to \infty}\sqrt{n}C \left\|\widehat{f}_n(\bmX) - f_0(\bmX)\right\|_2\p{\left\|\widehat{\zeta}_n(\bmx) - \zeta_0(\bmx)\right\|_2 + \left\|K\p{(\bmX_i - \bmx)/ h_n} - \xi_v\big(\bmX - \bmx\big)\right\|_2} = o_p(1).\end{equation}.}
